title: Joystick: read two axes and a button
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire the joystick's five pins, upload the sketch, and watch three numbers stream in Serial Monitor. Today is about seeing the module for what it is — two of the potentiometers you have already read, set at right angles, plus one push button underneath. Two analog reads become an (X, Y) position on a plane, one digital read catches the press, and every piece is one you have met before.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for the joystick module: VRX to GPIO 14, VRY to GPIO 13, SW to GPIO 12, the module's +5V pin fed from the 3.3V rail, and GND to ground, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: Joystick module
parts.explanation: Inside the joystick module are two potentiometers set at right angles — one follows left-right, the other up-down — plus a push button under the stick. X and Y arrive as ADC values; pressing straight down closes the button on the Z pin.

parts.name: 5 jumper wires (F/M)
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: Carries the X potentiometer's voltage for the board to measure.
Carries the Y potentiometer's voltage the same way.
The button under the stick pulls this pin LOW when you press.
Powers the module — the official diagram feeds this pin from the 3.3V rail.
Gives board and module the same zero point.

coachInstructions: Frame the module as a composite of things already learned — two potentiometers read with analogRead (the X and Y axes) and one push button read with digitalRead and an internal pull-up.
Land the 2D-position idea — X and Y alone are two dials, but together they name a point on a plane, which is how a stick steers.
Explain that each axis rests near 2048 but never exactly, so healthy resting values fall roughly between 1800 and 2200 and differ from board to board.
Guide one physical connection at a time and wait for learner confirmation before proceeding.
If one axis is stuck at 0 or 4095, check that axis's wire first — VRX belongs on GPIO 14 and VRY on GPIO 13.
If X and Y respond to the wrong direction of tilt, the VRX and VRY wires have traded places.
If Z never leaves 1, check the SW wire on GPIO 12 and coach a firm straight-down press.
If both axes read the same dead value, check the module's +5V and GND connections before touching code.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Push the five female jumper ends onto the module's header pins — the labels VRX, VRY, SW, +5V, and GND are printed beside them.
Connect VRX → GPIO 14, VRY → GPIO 13, and SW → GPIO 12.
Connect the module's +5V pin to the 3.3V rail and GND → GND, just as the chart shows.
Compare every wire to the chart before you plug in USB.
Open Sketch_13.1_Joystick.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Open Serial Monitor and set the baud rate to 115200.
Glide the stick to its edges, then press it straight down like a button.

arduino.explanation: One array holds all three pins — X on GPIO 14, Y on GPIO 13, Z on GPIO 12.
Sets the button pin so it rests at 1 and a press pulls it to 0.
Measures the X potentiometer's voltage as a number from 0 to 4095.
Prints all three values on one tab-separated line, twice a second.

micropython.explanation: Wraps GPIO 14 in an ADC reader — the same measurement analogRead does in C.
The button pin with the internal pull-up — 1 at rest, 0 while pressed.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: Two potentiometers at right angles turn the stick into an (X, Y) point on a plane, and a push button underneath reads the press — every part you have already met.
theoryModel.formula: one joystick = two ADC axes naming a point (X, Y) on a plane + one button (Z)

notes.title: Why the centre is near 2048, never exactly
notes.body: At rest each pot sits near the middle of its travel, so each axis reads near the middle of the 0-to-4095 range — about 2048. Real pots and the ADC never land dead centre, so expect resting numbers anywhere from about 1800 to 2200, each board its own. The centre is a neighbourhood, not a single value.

notes.title: Why the axes move independently
notes.body: The two pots share nothing but the stick. Tilt purely left-to-right and X swings while Y barely moves; tilt forward and Y swings while X holds. Because each axis is its own dial, the pair can point anywhere inside the square between the corners — that independence is what makes a plane rather than a line.

notes.title: Why Z rests at 1
notes.body: The internal pull-up holds the button pin HIGH with nothing else connected in the module. Pressing the stick closes the switch to ground, so the reading drops to 0 — a plain digital button carried alongside the two analog axes.

test.successCriteria: A new line lands twice a second. At rest both axes sit near mid-scale — around 2048, though real pots land anywhere from about 1800 to 2200, and your exact numbers will differ. A full push drives one axis toward 0 or 4095, and a straight-down press flips Z from 1 to 0.

firstChecks: Reseat that axis's wire — VRX belongs on GPIO 14, VRY on GPIO 13.
Check the SW wire on GPIO 12, then press straight down firmly — a sideways tilt won't click it.
Check the module's +5V and GND pins first.
Confirm 115200 baud, then the board and port.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Record the resting centre and notice it never lands on a clean 2048, push each axis to both extremes to find its real span, then read a few tilts as (X, Y) points to feel the plane the stick lives on.
